projects.title: Create Array and Access Elements
projects.description: Create an array of numbers. Log its length, access the first and last elements, and change an element by index.
projects.learningOutcome: Understanding array creation, length property, and indexing.
projects.logicExplanation: Arrays in JavaScript are zero‑indexed lists. You can create them using `[]` or `new Array()`. The `length` property gives the number of elements. Access elements by index, e.g., `arr[0]`.
projects.codeExplanation: This script creates an array of numbers, logs its length, accesses individual elements, and modifies an element by index.

projects.title: Push and Pop Demo
projects.description: Create an array of fruits. Use push to add a fruit, then pop to remove the last fruit. Log the array after each step.
projects.learningOutcome: Using mutating methods push and pop.
projects.logicExplanation: `push()` adds one or more elements to the end of an array and returns the new length. `pop()` removes the last element and returns it. Both modify the original array.
projects.codeExplanation: The code demonstrates `push()` and `pop()` by adding a new element to the end and removing the last one, printing the array after each operation.

projects.title: Shift and Unshift Demo
projects.description: Start with an array of numbers. Use unshift to add a number at the beginning, then shift to remove the first element. Log the array.
projects.learningOutcome: Using shift and unshift to manipulate array start.
projects.logicExplanation: `unshift()` adds elements to the beginning of an array, shifting existing elements to higher indices. `shift()` removes the first element and returns it, shifting the rest down.
projects.codeExplanation: The script uses `unshift()` to prepend a new element, then `shift()` to remove the first element, showing the array before and after.

projects.title: Splice Practice
projects.description: Given an array of months, use splice to remove two months starting from index 1, replace an element at index 3, and insert two new months at index 2 without deleting.
projects.learningOutcome: Mastering splice for removal, replacement, and insertion.
projects.logicExplanation: `splice()` is a versatile mutating method. It can remove, replace, or insert elements. Syntax: `array.splice(start, deleteCount, ...items)`.
projects.codeExplanation: This example demonstrates removing two elements starting at index 1, replacing an element, and inserting new elements without deletion.

projects.title: Slice and Concat
projects.description: Use slice to extract a portion of an array, and concat to merge two arrays. Ensure original arrays remain unchanged.
projects.learningOutcome: Using non‑mutating methods slice and concat.
projects.logicExplanation: `slice()` returns a shallow copy of a portion of an array without modifying the original. `concat()` merges two or more arrays into a new array.
projects.codeExplanation: The code creates a subarray using `slice()` and combines two arrays with `concat()`, proving that the original arrays remain unchanged.

projects.title: Searching with indexOf and includes
projects.description: Given an array of numbers, find the index of a specific number using indexOf, and check if a number exists using includes.
projects.learningOutcome: Using indexOf and includes for searching.
projects.logicExplanation: `indexOf()` returns the first index of a given element, or -1 if not found. `includes()` returns a boolean indicating whether the element exists in the array.
projects.codeExplanation: This script searches for a number in an array, logs its index if found, and checks for presence with `includes()`.

projects.title: Find and FindIndex
projects.description: From an array of numbers, use find to get the first even number, and findIndex to get its index.
projects.learningOutcome: Using find and findIndex with callbacks.
projects.logicExplanation: `find()` returns the first element that satisfies a testing function, or `undefined`. `findIndex()` returns the index of that element, or -1 if none satisfies.
projects.codeExplanation: The code finds the first even number in an array using `find()` and its index using `findIndex()`.

projects.title: For Loop Iteration
projects.description: Calculate the sum of all numbers in an array using a classic for loop.
projects.logicExplanation: A classic `for` loop allows iteration using an index variable, often from 0 to `array.length - 1`. It gives full control over the iteration process.
projects.codeExplanation: The script calculates the sum of array elements using a `for` loop and prints the result.

projects.title: For...of Loop
projects.description: Iterate over an array using for...of and log each element to the console.
projects.logicExplanation: `for...of` iterates over iterable values (like arrays) directly, giving each element value without needing an index. It's cleaner for simple iterations.
projects.codeExplanation: This example uses `for...of` to print each element of an array to the console.

projects.title: forEach Method
projects.description: Use forEach to log each element along with its index.
projects.logicExplanation: `forEach()` executes a provided function once for each array element. It's a higher‑order method that abstracts the loop and is commonly used for side effects.
projects.codeExplanation: The script uses `forEach()` to log each element and its index, demonstrating the method's callback.

projects.title: Map Method
projects.description: Use map to create a new array where each number is doubled.
projects.logicExplanation: `map()` creates a new array by applying a function to every element of the original array. It does not mutate the original array.
projects.codeExplanation: This code doubles each number in an array using `map()` and stores the result in a new array.

projects.title: Filter Method
projects.description: Use filter to extract all even numbers from an array.
projects.logicExplanation: `filter()` creates a new array with all elements that pass a test implemented by a function. It's useful for extracting subsets.
projects.codeExplanation: The script filters out all odd numbers, keeping only even numbers, and prints the filtered array.

projects.title: Reduce Method
projects.description: Use reduce to calculate the sum of all numbers in an array.
projects.logicExplanation: `reduce()` executes a reducer function on each element, resulting in a single output value. It can be used for summing, counting, grouping, etc.
projects.codeExplanation: This example sums all numbers in an array using `reduce()` and logs the total.

projects.title: Combined Map and Filter
projects.description: From an array of numbers, first filter out odd numbers, then map the even numbers to their squares.
projects.logicExplanation: Chaining higher‑order methods is common. Here we filter an array to keep only even numbers, then map each even to its square.
projects.codeExplanation: The code first filters evens, then maps them to squares, resulting in an array of squares of even numbers.

projects.title: Array of Objects Manipulation
projects.description: Given an array of user objects (name, age), extract the names of users older than 18 using filter and map.
projects.logicExplanation: When arrays contain objects, you often need to extract or transform properties. `map()` and `filter()` work with objects as well.
projects.codeExplanation: Given an array of user objects, the script extracts names of users older than 18 using `filter()` and `map()`.

projects.title: Remove Duplicates
projects.description: Write a function that removes duplicate elements from an array (preserving order) using filter and indexOf.
projects.logicExplanation: One way to remove duplicates is to use `filter()` with `indexOf()` to keep only the first occurrence of each element.
projects.codeExplanation: The code removes duplicate numbers from an array, returning a new array with unique values.

projects.title: Flatten Nested Arrays
projects.description: Write a function that flattens an array of arrays into a single array using reduce and concat.
projects.logicExplanation: Flattening a nested array means converting it to a single‑level array. This can be done with `reduce()` or the newer `flat()` method. Here we implement it manually.
projects.codeExplanation: The script uses `reduce()` and `concat()` to flatten an array of arrays into one array.

projects.title: Group By Property
projects.description: Given an array of people objects, group them by their age using reduce. The result should be an object where keys are ages and values are arrays of names.
projects.logicExplanation: Grouping objects by a property can be done with `reduce()`. The accumulator is an object where keys are the property values, and values are arrays of matching objects.
projects.codeExplanation: Given an array of people, the code groups them by age using `reduce()`.

projects.title: Chaining Methods
projects.description: Chain filter, map, and reduce to: filter numbers > 10, map them to their squares, then sum the squares.
projects.logicExplanation: You can chain array methods like `filter()`, `map()`, and `reduce()` to perform complex transformations in a concise, readable way.
projects.codeExplanation: The example filters numbers greater than 10, maps them to their squares, and sums the squares in one chain.

projects.title: Custom Sort
projects.description: Sort an array of objects (people) by age in ascending order using sort with a custom comparator.
projects.logicExplanation: `sort()` sorts an array in place. For objects, you provide a compare function. It mutates the original array.
projects.codeExplanation: The code sorts an array of objects (people) by age in ascending order using a custom comparator.
